title: Most parts of India likely to experience heatwave days between March & May: IMD - The Assam Tribune
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Assam
full_text:
Most parts of India likely to experience heatwave days between March & May: IMD
Most parts of India are likely to receive normal to above-normal rainfall in March, while the NE & some northwest and east-central regions may see below-normal rainfall.
Representational Image: Heatwave yet to be recognised a disaster in Assam (Photo: IANS)
New Delhi, Mar 1: The India Meteorological Department (IMD), in its monthly forecast issued on Saturday, said above-normal heatwave days are expected over most parts of the country between March and May.
Regions likely to witness above-normal heatwave conditions include West Rajasthan, Gujarat, Haryana, Punjab, southern and eastern Maharashtra, eastern Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Jharkhand, Gangetic West Bengal, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, and parts of north Karnataka and north Tamil Nadu.
“During the March-April-May (MAM) season, the increased likelihood of heatwave conditions may pose significant risks to public health, water resources, power demand, and essential services, particularly affecting vulnerable populations such as the elderly, children, outdoor workers, and individuals with pre-existing medical conditions,” IMD Director General Mrutyunjay Mohapatra said at a press conference.
However, during March, maximum temperatures are likely to remain normal to below normal over many parts of the country, except the Northeast and east India, and some parts of the Western Himalayan region and central and peninsular India.
Minimum temperatures are expected to remain normal over most parts of the country. Some areas of northwest India, the south peninsula and along the east coast may record normal to below-normal minimum temperatures during March.
Mohapatra said this could be because rainfall averaged over India is most likely to be normal in March 2026. The long period average (LPA) rainfall for March, based on data from 1971 to 2020, is about 29.9 mm.
While many parts of the country are expected to receive normal to above-normal rainfall in March, the Northeast and some parts of northwest and east-central India may witness below-normal rainfall.
The IMD also noted that weak La Niña conditions are currently prevailing over the equatorial Pacific. However, neutral El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) conditions are likely to return in the coming months, according to global models and the IMD’s Monsoon Mission Climate Forecast System.
Reviewing February’s weather, the IMD said rainfall across India last month was the lowest since 2001, and no cold waves or cold day conditions were recorded.
“The absence of any active western disturbances and lack of their interaction with easterly winds were the major reasons for subdued snowfall and rainfall during the month,” the IMD said.
Many parts of the country, except south peninsular and some parts of central India, recorded normal to above-normal maximum temperatures in February. Normal to above-normal minimum temperatures were also observed over many regions, except the eastern peninsula and east-central India.
“During February, India experienced the 10th highest maximum temperature, third highest minimum temperature, and fifth highest mean temperature since 1901,” the IMD added.

title: March to bring intense heat wave: Temperature may touch 40 degrees, Uttar Pradesh likely to face stronger s... - Bhaskar English
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Assam
full_text:
March to bring intense heat wave:Temperature may touch 40 degrees, Uttar Pradesh likely to face stronger summer than last year
Officially, the winter season is considered over from March 1st, and summer begins to knock. This time, with the beginning of March, concerns about the weather have increased. According to the India Meteorological Department (IMD), this year March could be among the warmest months ever.
Maximum and minimum temperatures in northern and northwestern states are expected to be significantly higher than normal. In March 2025, temperatures in many districts reached 35 to 38 degrees. This time, it could be 3-4 degrees higher than that.
In such a situation, the big question is, what will the weather be like in March? How much will the heat increase? How much will it affect crops? Read in this report
How has the weather been so far?
Mahesh Palawat, a weather expert at Noida-based Skymet Weather, explains - Uttar Pradesh experienced less cold this time compared to the last two years. In December-January, the average maximum and minimum temperatures were higher than normal. This time, extreme cold was not observed. Due to La Nia not being active, the cold was not severe.
Mohammad Danish, a scientist at the Lucknow Meteorological Center, also states the same. He says - this time, the activity of Western Disturbances remained low in the state during the winter season. Due to this, rainfall was also less than normal during the cold season.
This picture is from Ghaziabad on February 28. According to the Meteorological Department, temperatures are likely to rise after Holi.
How will the weather be in March
- According to weather expert Mahesh Palawat, this year March is likely to be warmer than usual. February also saw less cold compared to other years. This indicates that summer might arrive early and be intense this time.
- According to weather experts, temperatures across UP are expected to remain above normal. Not just the maximum, but the minimum temperature could also be higher than average. This will lead to warmth being felt both during the day and night.
- According to Mahesh Palawat, the El Nio condition is gradually developing. Due to this, there is a possibility of increased heat and a weaker monsoon. Intense heat may also occur in April. Heatwave-like conditions could develop.
- However, by the end of March, pre-monsoon activities may develop in western UP. Some areas might experience light storms or drizzles, but a significant drop in temperature is not expected from this.
- According to meteorologist Mohammad Danish, the maximum temperature in March is likely to remain around normal. However, the minimum temperature may be lower than normal. This could lead to a slight chill at night.
- He stated that there is a higher than normal probability of heatwave conditions (loo) in eastern UP. In such a situation, strong sunlight and hot winds may blow during the day. This could also affect daily life and crops.
Weather will change from the first week of March itself BHU meteorologist Manoj Srivastava says - The weather will start changing completely from the first week of March. The summer season will begin.
Both day and night temperatures will increase rapidly. By the last week of March, the maximum temperature in many districts of UP can reach 35 to 38 degrees Celsius.
IMD's warning about the weather
- Regarding the weather, the Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a warning that many parts of Northwest India may experience intense heat in the very first week of March.
- According to IMD, the daytime temperature in the plains of Northwest India may gradually increase by 2 to 4 degrees Celsius over the next 7 days. The maximum temperature is likely to remain 3 to 5 degrees Celsius above normal.
- This will directly affect UP as well. In most districts of UP, the temperature is already being recorded 3-5 degrees higher than normal. In the coming days, temperatures are expected to rise further in cities like Lucknow, Kanpur, Agra, Prayagraj, and Western UP.
- According to the Meteorological Department, a continuous increase in temperature will be observed in Delhi, Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, as well as in UP and the lower regions of Himachal-Uttarakhand. In such a situation, the effect of heat will be clearly felt in UP from the beginning of March.The picture is from Lucknow. There was bright sunshine here on February 28.
What will be the rain situation in March?
According to the Meteorological Department, there is a possibility of average normal rainfall across the country in March 2026. This means that on average, about 30 mm of rain occurs in the country in the month of March. This time too, rainfall is expected to be around this figure.
However, the weather will not be the same everywhere. Many parts of the country may experience normal or slightly above normal rainfall. But, some areas in Northeast India and Northwest and East-Central India are likely to receive below normal rainfall. There are also some areas where rainfall is generally very low in March anyway. Therefore, no significant change will be observed there.
Temperature remained above normal in February According to scientist Mohammad Danish, the maximum temperature recorded during the hottest day in February 2026 in UP was approximately 29-31 degrees or more. This was significantly higher than the normal February temperature.
In Kanpur, on February 23, the mercury reached about 29.6 degrees Celsius. This was reported as the hottest day of the season. This temperature was a record for that time, as such high temperatures are not usually recorded in February.
Similarly, on February 24, the maximum temperature in Varanasi was recorded at 31.5 degrees Celsius, which was very high.
Meteorological Department expressed concern over crops
- The Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) has predicted above-normal temperatures in March. This could affect the yield of major rabi crops like wheat and mustard.
- According to a senior scientist from the Meteorological Department, India could face one of its hottest March months this year.
- Experts say that February and March are crucial for grain filling and ripening of crops. In such a situation, an abnormal rise in temperature can affect the growth process of crops. Due to excessive heat, the grains do not develop completely. This raises concerns about a decrease in production.
- The continuously rising heat in March could reduce the total yield, whereas earlier, record production was expected.
- According to officials, maximum temperatures in some parts of Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, UP and Madhya Pradesh may rise up to 7 degrees Celsius above normal during March.
- These states contribute more than 80 percent of the country's total wheat and mustard production. In such a situation, if there is a sharp increase in temperature, its effect can also be seen on food grain production at the national level.
How will March be for crops?
According to BHU agricultural scientist Professor PK Singh, the month of March is considered a crucial time for Rabi crops (wheat, barley, gram, peas). However, the possibility of higher-than-normal temperatures this time has increased farmers' concerns.
PK Singh says, "According to the Meteorological Department, temperatures could be 3 to 5 degrees Celsius higher than average. This is likely to have a direct impact on crops. Wheat is currently in the grain formation and ripening stage. If temperatures rise rapidly, the grains may not develop fully. This could lead to a decrease in production."
If intense heat occurs prematurely, the crop ripens quickly and the size of the grains remains small. The mustard crop is also in its final stage. Gram and other pulse crops are also likely to be adversely affected by the heat.

title: Govt issues heatstroke warning - Bangkok Post
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
full_text:
PUBLISHED : 1 Mar 2026 at 05:42
People, particularly vulnerable groups, those with chronic illnesses and outdoor workers, are being urged to take precautions against heat stroke, as temperatures are forecast to rise above last year's levels.
Ayrin Phanrit, deputy government spokeswoman, said temperatures could reach 42–43 degrees Celsius in high-risk areas, including the provinces of Mae Hong Son, Lampang and Tak, so the public should avoid prolonged stays outdoors.
People are advised to limit outdoor activities between 11am and 3pm, drink water frequently and wear "comfort clothing" to reduce the risk of heat stroke, which can be fatal.
High-risk groups include the elderly, infants and young children, people with chronic diseases, such as heart disease, lung disease and obesity, and those working outdoors. Heat stroke occurs when the body overheats and affects vital systems.
"Avoid staying out in the heat for a prolonged period of time. Also, avoid consuming alcohol and do not leave anyone in the car [unattended], especially small children," said Ms Ayrin.
Meanwhile, the Meteorological Department said storms are forecast from Tuesday to Friday, beginning in the Northeast before affecting the East, Central Plains, Bangkok and surrounding areas, and the North.
Air pollution levels remain low to moderate due to moderate ventilation and expected rainfall, the department said.

title: Tyler Reif Released From Medical Facility After Treatment For Heat Exhaustion - TobyChristie.com
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
full_text:
Tyler Reif, who scored a 16th-place finish in Saturday’s NASCAR Craftsman Truck Series OnlyBulls Green Flag 150 on the streets of St. Petersburg, has been released from a local medical facility after undergoing treatment for heat exhaustion, according to a statement from Niece Motorsports following the event.
“Tyler Reif has been released from a St. Petersburg area medical facility after being treated for heat exhaustion,” the team said in a medical update at 8:02 PM ET. “Tyler and his family would like to express their gratitude to the NASCAR officials, track medical workers, and the local medical staff for their care.”
Niece Motorsports sent out an initial statement at 4:39 PM ET stating that Reif had been transported to a local area medical facility following Saturday afternoon’s OnlyBulls Green Flag 150 on the streets of St. Petersburg.
“Following today’s NASCAR Craftsman Truck Series event at St. Petersburg, Tyler Reif, the driver of the No. 42 Chevrolet for Niece Motorsports, was transported to an area medical facility for further observation,” the team stated.
Reif, 18, was making his third career start in the NASCAR Craftsman Truck Series. The native of Henderson, Nevada, finished 17th a week ago at EchoPark Speedway.
Prior to his move to the NASCAR National Series ranks, Reif scored an ARCA Menards Series win at Phoenix Raceway in 2023, to go along with three wins in the ARCA Menards Series West, where he would also finish second in the championship battle in 2023 and 2024.
Reif also finished runner-up in the 2025 ARCA Menards Series East championship fight, after scoring four top-fives and five top-10s during the eight-race schedule.
Toby Christie
Toby is the Founder of TobyChristie.com, and Editor-in-Chief of Racing America. Toby is also the co-host of The Final Lap Weekly Podcast. Additionally, he is an award-winning writer, and has followed the sport as a fan since 1993.
